lens.included_activities: US operators that preserve and exhibit historically significant sites, buildings, forts, communities, archaeological sites, battlefields, historical ships, and pioneer villages and repeatedly serve visitors, members, schools, tour groups, renters, or sponsors.
lens.excluded_activities: Government-run sites; nonprofit or institutionally captive sites without transferable equity control; passive owners of historic real estate; preservation contractors; ordinary lodging, event, or retail businesses located in historic buildings; museums and natural parks classified outside 712120; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The coherent transferable-firm population is the small for-profit subset of historical-site operators. Revenue commonly comes from posted-price admissions, tours, memberships or season passes, education programs, special events, venue rental, merchandise, food, sponsorship, and sometimes donations or grants.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to for-profit operating businesses because the history field is dominated by government-private-nonprofit partnerships whose governance cannot undergo a conventional equity-control transfer; passive historic-property ownership is also excluded because it does not provide the screened visitor service.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 712100 occupation mix rather than 712120 alone. In that population, tour and travel guides are 9.02% of employment, security guards 3.76%, sales occupations 7.96%, cashiers 3.13%, educational/library occupations 14.04%, archivists/curators/museum technicians 8.84%, and arts/design/media occupations 4.40%. Census BTOS identifies writing, document analysis, and information search as leading generative-AI tasks. I map high exposure to research, interpretation and education drafts, marketing, fundraising, reservations, scheduling, and administration; moderate exposure to guided interpretation, ticketing, retail, and visitor inquiries; and low exposure to preservation trades, site maintenance, security, live performance, and physical operations. Historical sites likely have more grounds, preservation, and live-guiding work than the broader subsector, so the 712100 mix may overstate exposure; wage weighting toward managers and knowledge staff partly offsets that error.
a.rationale: AI can materially assist narrative research and drafting, school materials, translation, audience communications, reservations, CRM work, and routine back-office processing. The historically specific setting, live human interpretation, preservation, building and grounds work, security, events, and hospitality keep most labor outside full substitution.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 712120 payroll-by-SOC extract joined to O*NET task frequencies, plus time-and-motion interviews across interpretation, preservation, visitor services, events, marketing, development, and finance at 20 commercial historical attractions, would materially change a.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupation mix at NAICS 712100 and includes museums, zoos, gardens, and nature parks as well as historical sites.
AI exposure does not establish safe substitution where contested history, source provenance, cultural sensitivity, accessibility, and public trust require human judgment.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and a harmonization divisor, so vintage and basis differences remain material.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS covers all US nonfarm employer businesses, not historical sites: 18% used AI in a business function in Nov. 2025-Jan. 2026, 22% expected use within six months, 57% of users operated in three or fewer functions, 66% used AI only to augment tasks, and 2% reported AI-related employment decreases. I extend adoption over five years but discount for small site budgets, seasonal staffing, legacy collections or reservation systems, limited structured data, factual and reputational review, and preservation obligations. Packaged visitor-service and back-office tools could make historical sites catch up to the general business population, while small-scale nonprofit-derived operating practices and bespoke interpretation could slow realized substitution.
rho.rationale: The implementable core is human-reviewed research, content adaptation, translation, communications, marketing, fundraising, scheduling, reservations, and routine administration. Accurate contextual interpretation, sensitive narratives, live engagement, physical preservation, maintenance, and safety require staff, and many eligible operators may lack the systems and scale needed to redesign workflows quickly.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal cohort comparing paid hours, avoided hires, output, error correction, visitor satisfaction, and incidents before and after AI deployment in ticketing, interpretation, education, marketing, events, and finance would materially move rho.
caveats: BTOS measures any business-function use, not the share of exposed historical-site labor implemented as substitution or avoided hiring.
The five-year conversion is judgmental; political controversy, incorrect interpretation, copyright claims, or trust failures could impose tighter review.

e.rationale: AASLH's census describes the history field as a deep partnership between government and private nonprofits, and more than 80% of private nonprofit history organizations have revenue below $200,000. IMLS likewise describes the museum field, including historic houses and sites, as slightly more than 70% private nonprofit with most of the balance publicly owned. Of the frozen LMM-band firms, only commercial visitor attractions with transferable operating control, sustainable recurring demand, and usable rights to the site, interpretation, and ancillary operations qualify. The absence of a current 712120 tax-status split makes a bounded estimate more honest than treating broad governance evidence as a direct measure.
e.change_evidence: Link the 1,120 employer establishments in the 2023 Census profile to Economic Census tax status, IRS exempt-organization records, Secretary of State legal form, ultimate owners, normalized EBITDA, property/lease control, and restrictions on preservation easements or collections.
caveats: History-organization evidence includes museums, historical societies, and other entities outside NAICS 712120 and does not quantify for-profit historical sites directly.

The frozen n=23 is an ESTIMATE using size-class receipts and a recreation-sector EBITDA margin; contributions, restricted funds, deferred maintenance, volunteer labor, and owned real estate can make normalized EBITDA especially misleading.
Some nominally private sites may be burdened by preservation covenants, donated-property restrictions, public leases, or inseparable real estate that prevents a qualifying transfer.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were age 55 or older in 2018, and SBA's Census-based synthesis reports that 64.5% of current owners planned to sell their businesses. I translate these broad owner demographics and eventual intentions into a five-year transfer probability, discounting for timing, failed sales, closures, internal succession, and asset-only deals. Eligible commercial historic attractions may be unusually founder- or family-led, increasing retirement pressure, but singular real estate, preservation obligations, seasonal earnings, and key-person storytelling can reduce buyer demand and completed control transfers.
s5.rationale: General small-business succession pressure is relevant to the few eligible commercial sites, but no observed denominator or closed-deal series exists for 712120. A moderate range recognizes likely aging ownership while excluding closures, nonprofit conversions, family gifts without arm's-length control acquisition, and real-estate sales that do not preserve the operation.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of 712120 control transactions from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) acquisition loans, county deed records, and attraction brokers, coded against a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change s5.
caveats: The owner evidence is economy-wide, older, and not specific to historical sites or the $1-10M EBITDA band.
Planned sales are not completed transfers and may occur beyond five years.
A site or property sale may extinguish the operating visitor service and is therefore not automatically a qualifying transfer.

q.rationale: The Census product definition identifies single-visit and multiple-visit admissions, special-event surcharges, memberships, guided tours, and food or beverage benefits as historical-site products. These are generally posted-price rather than cost-plus contracts, permitting an operator to retain much of an implemented productivity benefit initially. Over five years, local attraction competition, admission affordability, staff wage pressure, maintenance backlogs, and the need to reinvest in preservation, interpretation, and events share part of the benefit with visitors and employees.
q.change_evidence: Five-year post-automation cohorts with real revenue per visit, ticket and membership renewal elasticity, labor cost, preservation capex, deferred maintenance, exhibit or event reinvestment, and competitor price changes would materially change q.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled gross benefits at for-profit historical sites.
Site economics vary sharply between destination attractions, seasonal living-history villages, ships, houses, battlefields, and local sites.
Preservation obligations can absorb savings even when customers do not receive an explicit price concession.

d5.bridge: AASLH's 2023 survey of 663 history organizations found in-person visits up 37% in 2022, but most remained below pre-pandemic levels; institutions with annual budgets of $1-10 million still lagged their 2010s visitation, with the $5-10 million group at only 56% of its 2013 visits. NPS recorded 323 million recreation visits in 2025, 2.7% below the 2024 record; national historic sites represented 2% and national historical parks 9% of visits. I treat these government and nonprofit-heavy visitation observations as proxies for current commercial historical-site quantity and center five-year constant-quality demand near flat with material upside and downside. Free public sites may compete with commercial sites and bias their trends upward or downward depending on local complementarity, while pandemic recovery changes are not structural growth.
d5.rationale: Historical-place demand has durable educational, family, school, tourism, commemorative, and civic uses but is mature, seasonal, and exposed to travel, household budgets, weather, and program relevance. The 2026 national semiquincentennial may create a temporary boost rather than sustained year-five growth. Good programming and events support upside; stale interpretation, deferred maintenance, controversy, climate damage, or tourism weakness support downside.
d5.change_evidence: A representative 712120 panel of paid admissions, repeat visits, memberships, school groups, events, real revenue, closures, and capacity from 2019 through 2026, plus forward bookings and local tourism data, would materially change d5.
caveats: AASLH covers history organizations of many types and governance forms, while NPS is public and includes many park units outside NAICS 712120.
Attendance is an imperfect proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service quantity because free access, event mix, pricing, and visit duration vary.
The latest detailed AASLH survey reflects 2022 pandemic recovery rather than the 2026 starting level.

o.rationale: The defining product is access to and interpretation of a particular preserved place. AASLH found that virtual programming was offered by 43% of surveyed history organizations in 2022 and described it as a complement rather than a replacement for traditional in-person engagement. Digital tours, AI guides, and self-service ticketing can eliminate some transactions and reach remote audiences, but an accountable operator remains necessary to preserve structures and landscapes, manage safety and access, establish interpretive authority, run events, and maintain the place. Nearly all surviving year-five physical service quantity should therefore remain operator-required.
o.change_evidence: Paired visitor studies measuring substitution between paid physical visits and virtual or AI-guided experiences, along with site-level staffing, unattended access, preservation incidents, insurance requirements, event utilization, and digital-only revenue, would materially change o.
caveats: No source directly measures year-five operator-required quantity for commercial historical sites.
Virtual access may substitute more strongly for small interiors, archives, or low-differentiation sites than for immersive destination properties.
AI-driven labor changes are counted in rho and not again as elimination of operator-required demand.

narrative.executive_view: Historical sites combine a high labor share with some AI-addressable interpretation and administration, but much of the work is physical, live, or place-specific. More importantly, the field's government-nonprofit structure leaves few transferable commercial operators, and the frozen LMM count may be distorted by contributed revenue, volunteer labor, real estate, and deferred preservation costs.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate source discovery, first drafts of interpretive material, educational adaptation, translation, accessibility content, visitor inquiries, marketing, fundraising, scheduling, reservations, CRM upkeep, and routine finance or HR work. It can also support self-guided audio or visual experiences. Staff remain necessary to verify contested historical claims and provenance, engage visitors, preserve buildings and landscapes, run events, secure sites, maintain facilities, and accept accountability for accurate and sensitive interpretation.
narrative.value_capture: Commercial sites typically use posted admissions, tours, memberships, events, rentals, retail, food, and sponsorship rather than contractual cost pass-through, which permits meaningful initial retention of productivity gains. Retention erodes through affordability constraints, competition from free public sites and other leisure options, employee wage pressure, and the need to reinvest in preservation, maintenance, programming, and accessibility.
narrative.firm_availability: The 2022 history census characterizes the field as a government-private-nonprofit partnership and shows that most nonprofit organizations are very small. A commercial equity-control screen therefore excludes most operators. Even among seemingly eligible sites, title or lease terms, preservation easements, restricted donations, local-government relationships, founder dependence, and the inseparability of land from operations can block transferability. General owner-aging evidence suggests succession events, but no historical-site deal-flow series exists.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand for experiencing authentic places is culturally and educationally durable, but visitation is seasonal, tourism-sensitive, and uneven across institutions. Pandemic recovery data showed strong rebounds without full recovery for mid-sized history organizations, while 2025 NPS traffic eased from a record. Virtual programs broaden access but have behaved mainly as complements. A site that stops refreshing interpretation or funding maintenance can lose demand quickly despite the underlying relevance of history.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: A bad outcome can arise from an effectively empty transferable target population, hidden preservation capex, property or artifact title defects, government or donor restrictions, seasonal and weather-driven traffic, founder-dependent storytelling, weak repeat visitation, community controversy, or inaccurate AI interpretation that damages trust. The recreation-sector margin bridge may be especially unreliable because volunteer labor, grants, restricted funds, owned property, and deferred maintenance make reported operating economics unlike ordinary attractions.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can improve research, interpretation production, visitor communications, and administration while authentic place-based demand remains operator-dependent.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most historical sites are public or nonprofit and the few commercial sites can be inseparable from restricted real estate, preservation obligations, and founder-specific operations.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 712100
sources.supports: BLS reports the broader subsector occupation mix, including tour and travel guides at 9.02% of employment, security guards 3.76%, sales occupations 7.96%, cashiers 3.13%, educational/library occupations 14.04%, archivists/curators/museum technicians 8.84%, and arts/design/media occupations 4.40%.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.supports: Census reports 18% firm AI use in Nov. 2025-Jan. 2026, 22% expected use within six months, limited functional breadth, writing/document analysis/information search as leading tasks, 66% augmentation-only use, and employment decreases at 2% of firms.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS data from Dec. 2025-May 2026 show overall AI use around 17-20%, expected use around 20-23%, greater use among larger firms, and less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees.

sources.title: AASLH Releases 2022 National Census of History Organizations
sources.supports: AASLH identified 21,588 US history organizations, characterized the field as a government-private-nonprofit partnership, and reported that more than 80% of private nonprofit history organizations had revenue below $200,000.

sources.title: IMLS Keynote Address on the Museum Landscape
sources.supports: IMLS states that slightly more than 70% of US museums, explicitly including historic houses and sites, were private nonprofits and the remainder were publicly owned by governments or public universities.

sources.title: Census Bureau Profile: 712120 Historical Sites
sources.supports: The 2023 County Business Patterns profile reports 1,120 employer establishments in NAICS 712120.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey visualization reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in data year 2018.

sources.title: Paths to Business Ownership
sources.supports: SBA's Census-based fact sheet reports that 64.5% of current owners planned to sell their businesses and 22% of employer-business owners had purchased their business.

sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 712
sources.supports: Census defines historical-site admissions as single- or multiple-occasion access, special-event surcharges, and membership fees, potentially including guided tours, games, rides, food, and beverages.

sources.title: 2023 National Visitation Report
sources.supports: AASLH's survey of 663 history organizations found visits up 37% in 2022 but most still below pre-pandemic levels; $5-10 million-budget organizations reached only 56% of their 2013 visits, and virtual programming remained complementary.

sources.title: National Park Service Visitor Use Data
sources.supports: NPS reports 323 million recreation visits in 2025, down 2.7% from the 2024 record; national historic sites represented 2% and national historical parks 9% of visits.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 712120 Historical Sites
sources.supports: Census defines the industry as preserving and exhibiting sites, buildings, forts, or communities of particular historical interest, including archaeological sites, battlefields, historical ships, and pioneer villages.

sources.title: Authenticity and Authority: The Role of Historic Sites
sources.supports: AASLH reports that 81% of respondents in a 2018 sample considered history museums and historic sites absolutely or somewhat trustworthy and emphasizes the authority created by authentic places and careful primary-source research.
